topic: The Eclipse Prediction: Genuine Test or Pre-Fitted?
our_argument_summary: SEC-6.7 frames the dome's August 2026 eclipse prediction as 'the most falsifiable prediction the dome model has made' while identifying four structural problems: (1) no mechanism derivation from dome geometry, (2) globe physics (Chapman Sq current) predicts the same range, (3) two prior eclipse tests failed (WIN-025 REMOVED, PROS-003 SUSPENDED), (4) the prediction window is wide (−17 to −21 nT = 4 nT range ≈ ±10.5%). The section credits the pre-registration and specificity, then redirects to Section 4.2 for the full dual-baseline and FSF analysis.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: Eclipse prediction uses ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF. Two baselines: BOU (-10.9 nT, 2017 real observation) and W004 (-22.24 nT, repurposed noise-floor measurement). FSF values are hardcoded per station; monitor.py references indicate ecm_eclipse = -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 with all literals hardcoded.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Entirely hardcoded. The dome's monitor.py uses static literals for baseline, coverage, and FSF. No runtime computation from dome geometric parameters. The fsf_derivation.py script exists as an offline curve-fitting exercise, not as a live derivation.
data_sources_used: Besselian eclipse elements from JPL DE440/441 (globe-sourced)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Kp < 2 quiet-day filter voids the test when geomagnetic activity is elevated. monitor.py records pass=null (not pass=false) for disturbed conditions, creating a non-result rather than a failure.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: SEC-6.7 itself doesn't make direct code claims — it defers to Section 4.2 for code analysis. The code claims in 4.2 (hardcoded FSF, line 512 reference) were reviewed in SEC-4.2.c2.json.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: monitor.py eclipse entry uses ecm_eclipse = -18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 with all three values as hardcoded literals (per Section 4.2 text citing line 512). No external API fetch for eclipse observation data. The Kp filter is applied but the 'observation' is static.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Chapman (1933) Sq current mechanism predicts 5–25 nT eclipse magnetic perturbations at INTERMAGNET stations. The dome renames ionospheric conductivity reduction as 'aetheric field disruption' while predicting the same magnitude range. Meza et al. (2021, arXiv:2107.12327) match observed signals 'strikingly accurately.'
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: BOU baseline (-10.9 nT) is the 2017 eclipse measurement itself — a known value at registration time. W004 baseline (-22.24 nT) comes from a weekly test result available before registration. FSF values are derived from fitting to existing station data. Only the 2026 eclipse event itself is prospective; the prediction formula inputs are all post-hoc.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: FSF = H(r)/r reduces algebraically to tan(lat)/tan(ref_lat) as shown in Section 4.2's verification. The dome geometry (H(r) function shape) cancels out completely. Baseline comes from BOU 2017 (globe-era INTERMAGNET). Coverage comes from Besselian elements (globe ephemerides). No dome-specific parameter enters the numerical prediction.

holes_found.description: The '−17 to −21 nT at Ebro' attribution cannot be verified from the dome's raw predictions text. The dome's predictions page (raw-text/03-predictions.txt) shows Ebro BOU = −10.7 nT and Ebro W004 = −21.7 nT. The range '−17 to −21' doesn't match either baseline prediction for Ebro. It falls between the two baselines (the BOU upper error bound is −12.8 nT, the W004 lower error bound is −19.6 nT). If this comes from the dome's homepage, the raw text extraction (01-home.txt) doesn't contain this specific characterization. Either the quote is from a different dome page version, or it's a paraphrase being presented as a quote. A dome defender could accuse us of misquoting.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the exact source of '−17 to −21 nT at Ebro.' If it's from a page version not in our raw-text archive, add the source URL and extraction date. If it's our interpretation of the dual-baseline range, rewrite as 'the dome's two baselines produce Ebro predictions of −10.7 and −21.7 nT respectively' rather than presenting a range that doesn't match either prediction.

holes_found.description: The '4 nT range (±10.5%)' critique in point 4 is misleading in context. SEC-6.7 says the headline prediction is a '4 nT range' but in the same paragraph acknowledges 'approximately −5 to −26 nT with error bars across both baselines.' The section uses the narrow Ebro headline characterization for the ±10.5% critique while the actual prediction structure spans 21.2 nT (or 26.4 nT with error bars). A defender could say: 'you're attacking our tightest prediction (Ebro) while ignoring that we gave precise per-station predictions.' The real vulnerability is the FULL range covering nearly all plausible quiet-day signals — which is Section 4.2's hedging argument, not the 4 nT headline.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) drop the '4 nT range' critique since it applies only to the headline and is actually the dome's STRONGEST framing (narrow, specific, falsifiable), or (b) explicitly contrast it: 'The headline says −17 to −21 nT (a 4 nT window), but the full dual-baseline structure spans −5 to −26 nT across all stations — covering nearly the entire plausible quiet-day signal space (see Section 4.2).' This makes the hedging structure visible without misrepresenting the headline prediction.

holes_found.description: SEC-6.7 point 3 mentions WIN-025 REMOVED and PROS-003 SUSPENDED but omits that WIN-010 itself was ALSO REMOVED in the same March 2026 batch action (per FAIL-006 in uncounted-failures.json) and then re-confirmed by the dome using a different eclipse with quieter conditions. This removal-reinstatement cycle is significant: the dome removed its baseline measurement, then reinstated it, then built a third prediction attempt on top of it. A dome defender reading our text would say 'WIN-010 was never removed' — but it was, temporarily. The pattern of remove → reinstate → predict again → wider criteria strengthens the 'third attempt' argument but we don't use it.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to point 3: 'WIN-010 itself was removed in the same March 2026 audit (FAIL-006) before being reinstated with a different eclipse dataset as confirmation. The dome's baseline measurement has thus been through a removal-reinstatement cycle — the very observation now serving as the BOU baseline input was previously withdrawn as unreliable.'

holes_found.description: SEC-6.7 says 'Across seven stations with different coverage percentages' but the dome predicts at NINE stations (E001–E009). The section earlier correctly says '9 named INTERMAGNET stations.' The '7 stations' reference is internally inconsistent.

The section's tone shifts between 'credit where due' (paragraph 2) and the structural problems (paragraphs 3–6). The credit paragraph is genuinely charitable — 'the most falsifiable prediction the dome model has made' — but point 1 then says 'the prediction is a number — not a consequence of the model,' which somewhat contradicts the credit. A more consistent framing would acknowledge the prediction's specificity while noting it doesn't derive from the model.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften point 1 to: 'While the prediction is specific and falsifiable, the dome site does not show a derivation from dome geometry to this specific magnitude. The number is stated, not derived from ovoid cavity parameters.' This preserves the criticism while staying consistent with the credit paragraph.

holes_found.description: The cross-reference to Section 4.2 says 'See Section 4.2 for the full dual-baseline structure and discriminating test analysis' with an onclick that shows the 'pages' tab. This is correct post-restructure (Section 4.2 is in Part 4, which is on the 'pages' tab). Verified consistent.
holes_found.recommendation: No change needed. Cross-reference is correct.

stronger_arguments.description: SEC-6.7 could explicitly note the denominator problem: the dome counts WIN-010 (BOU 2017 observation) as a confirmed WIN, then uses that same observation as the baseline input for predicting the 2026 eclipse. If the 2026 prediction confirms, the dome will have used one observation to generate two WINs. This circular counting is a structural flaw the section doesn't mention — Section 4.2 covers it under 'circularly calibrated' but SEC-6.7 doesn't flag it.

The section could note that the dome's evolution page (raw-text/06-evolution.txt line 21) states early versions 'Incorrectly predicted 2024 eclipse magnitudes identically across all latitudes.' This self-acknowledged failure — predicting identical magnitudes when magnitudes clearly vary — shows the current latitude-dependent FSF formula was developed AFTER the 2024 data revealed the earlier formula's failure. The FSF formula is thus a retroactive fix, not a first-principles derivation. This strengthens the 'pre-fitted' side of the 'genuine test or pre-fitted?' framing.

kernel_of_truth.description: The eclipse prediction IS genuinely the dome model's most falsifiable and best-documented prediction. The pre-registration with Git SHA timestamps and OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring is real and verifiable. The per-station structure with specific magnitudes, coverage values, and error bars goes far beyond typical alternative cosmology claims. The dome has also shown intellectual honesty by removing previous eclipse claims that failed (WIN-025, PROS-003, temporarily WIN-010). The 'credit where due' paragraph in SEC-6.7 appropriately acknowledges this.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Specificity and pre-registration are necessary but not sufficient for scientific legitimacy. The prediction must also (a) derive from the model's own parameters and (b) predict something the competing model cannot. FSF = tan(lat) by algebraic identity, so the dome geometry contributes nothing to the prediction. The BOU baseline is a globe-era observation. The coverage values come from globe ephemerides. The prediction is specific and timestamped, but its content is globe physics relabeled. If it confirms, both models predicted it; if it fails, only one model fails. The prediction's quality is process-quality (good registration practices) not content-quality (novel physics).

advocate_mode.best_defense: The dome defender's strongest rebuttal: 'Your entire section is premature and presumptuous. You're dismissing a prediction BEFORE it has been tested. You acknowledge it's the most falsifiable prediction we've made, you acknowledge the pre-registration is genuine, and you acknowledge the specific per-station structure. Yet your conclusion is that it's structurally flawed — before a single data point from August 2026 exists. Real science waits for data. Furthermore, you claim FSF = tan(lat), but that's only true if our station placement formula r × tan(lat) = H(r) is itself correct — and we claim it IS correct because it derives from dome geometry. You're calling a geometric consequence a tautology. If our H(r) function is wrong, the station placements would be wrong, and the predictions would fail. The test is whether the FSF values produce correct magnitude predictions at 9 independent stations — not whether FSF can be algebraically simplified. Finally, your two prior failure claims (WIN-025, PROS-003) actually demonstrate scientific rigor — we removed them rather than counting them. The 2026 prediction has stricter methodology precisely because we learned from those failures. That's the scientific method working.'
advocate_mode.preemptive_recommendation: The 'premature dismissal' argument has rhetorical force. Add a sentence after the 'credit where due' paragraph: 'We assess the prediction's structure and provenance now — before the test — precisely because post-hoc assessment is what we criticize the dome model for. Our structural concerns (non-discriminating range, globe-sourced inputs, FSF tautology) do not depend on the August 2026 result. If the data confirms the prediction, our Section 4.2 commitment specifies exactly what would change our verdict.' This preempts the 'wait for data' objection by explaining WHY pre-test structural analysis is legitimate — it's the same standard we apply to the dome.

cross_win_consistency.inconsistencies_found: No inconsistency. All three related WINs carry 'Std Model Explains' verdicts with consistent reasoning (Chapman Sq mechanism, globe-sourced baselines, no dome derivation). WIN-010 detail text mentions the Kp asymmetry (dome accepts Kp 3-4 BOU baseline as WIN but requires Kp < 2 for 2026 test), which SEC-6.7 doesn't explicitly repeat — but this is covered in Section 4.2 and doesn't contradict SEC-6.7's framing. WIN-025 REMOVED status is correctly referenced in SEC-6.7 point 3. WIN-068 triple-counting is not mentioned in SEC-6.7 (it's a WIN-level concern, not a section-level one). Cross-WIN arguments are compatible.

claims_checked: -17 to -21 nT is a 4 nT range (±10.5%)
quantitative_verification.computations: Computed ΔZ = baseline × coverage × FSF for all 9 stations with both baselines. Verified FSF = tan(lat)/tan(40°) matches published values to within 0.003 for 8/9 stations (Canary Islands differs by 0.016). Verified BOU range [-12.8, -5.0] and W004 range [-26.2, -10.3]. Computed ±10.5% for the -17 to -21 range: midpoint = -19, half-spread = 2, 2/19 = 10.5%. Verified station count: 9 stations (section incorrectly says 'seven' in point 4).
quantitative_verification.discrepancies: One numerical error: point 4 says 'Across seven stations' but there are nine stations. One unverifiable claim: '-17 to -21 nT at Ebro' does not match either baseline prediction for Ebro (BOU = -10.7, W004 = -21.7). All other numerical claims verified correct.

deeper_analysis:
SEC-6.7 serves as the predictions-page framing for the eclipse prediction, complementing Section 4.2's detailed scientific analysis. Its role is to ask 'is this a genuine test or a pre-fitted number?' — a structural question about prediction methodology rather than a physics argument.

The section is well-structured and appropriately charitable in its opening credit. The four structural problems are valid. However, three moderate issues weaken it:

1. The '-17 to -21 nT at Ebro' attribution appears unverifiable from our raw text archive, creating a citation vulnerability.

2. The '4 nT range' critique actually attacks the dome's STRONGEST possible framing (a narrow, specific prediction) rather than its weakest (the full 21.2 nT dual-baseline range). A dome defender would prefer us to focus on the headline because it makes the prediction look specific and falsifiable. Our real target should be the dual-baseline hedging structure that Section 4.2 dissects — SEC-6.7 should point there more forcefully.

3. The WIN-010 removal-reinstatement cycle is missing, which would strengthen the 'third attempt' argument by showing that even the baseline measurement was once deemed unreliable.

The advocate defense scores 3/5 because the 'wait for data' argument is genuinely compelling — we ARE assessing a prediction before its test date, which is rhetorically unusual. Our preemptive commitment in Section 4.2 partially addresses this, but SEC-6.7 doesn't reference that commitment.

citations_verified: Curto et al. (2006) — cited for eclipse magnetic effects; standard reference in solar-quiet variation literature
Malin et al. (2000) — cited for eclipse magnetic effects; well-known eclipse magnetic study
citations_unchecked: OpenTimestamps blockchain anchoring (2026-03-22) — date not independently verified against Bitcoin blockchain; would require checking the OTS proof file against the dome's git repository
Dome homepage '-17 to -21 nT at Ebro' — cannot verify from available raw text archive
